story_title: Germany Repatriates Its Gold — A 4-Year Mission to Bring 300 Tonnes Home
summary: After Germany's federal audit court demanded the Bundesbank physically inspect its 3,384-tonne gold reserve — much of it stored at the New York Fed and the Bank of England since the Cold War — the central bank launched a five-year repatriation program that returned 674 tonnes by 2017, three years ahead of schedule, at zero cost to taxpayers.
recommended_arc: Hook: Germany's auditors discover the Bundesbank had never physically counted its own gold stored in New York. Rising: political pressure mounts; conspiracy theories proliferate. Climax: Bundesbank announces a 5-year repatriation — then finishes in 4. Payoff: Germany now holds more than half its gold at home for the first time since the Cold War.

verified_facts.claim: As of 2012, Germany held approximately 3,391 tonnes of gold, making it the world's second-largest national gold reserve after the United States; about 45 percent was stored at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, 13 percent at the Bank of England in London, and 11 percent at the Banque de France in Paris.

In January 2013, the Bundesbank announced it would repatriate a total of 674 tonnes of gold by 2020 — 300 tonnes from New York and 374 tonnes from Paris — to move more than 50 percent of Germany's gold reserves to Frankfurt.

The Bundesbank completed the repatriation three years ahead of schedule; it announced in August 2017 that the full 674-tonne transfer was complete, with all Paris holdings fully returned and 300 tonnes returned from New York.

The German Federal Court of Auditors (Bundesrechnungshof) had issued a report in 2012 criticising the Bundesbank for never physically inspecting its gold stored abroad, which it called 'unacceptable,' providing the political catalyst for the repatriation program.

The gold was transported in multiple shipments over four years using armoured vehicles and secure freight aircraft; the Bundesbank said the operation cost nothing additional to the taxpayer as security and transport costs were handled within existing contracts.

Following the repatriation, Germany holds 1,710 tonnes (50.6 percent) of its gold in Frankfurt, 1,236 tonnes (36.6 percent) in New York, and 432 tonnes (12.8 percent) in London; the Paris allocation was fully repatriated.
